% Core assignment predicate: flier F has month M and charm C
assign(F, M, C)

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{F1=F2; M1=M2; C1=C2}=0 :- assign(F1,M1,C1), assign(F2,M2,C2), (F1,M1,C1)!=(F2,M2,C2).

% 1. The passenger with the wishbone is either Troy or Neal.
{F="Troy"; F="Neal"}=1 :- assign(F,M,C), C="wishbone".

% 2. The four fliers are the passenger with the rabbit's foot, the passenger leaving in April, Yolanda and the passenger leaving in February.
% This implies that the four fliers are uniquely identified by these four roles.
% So: Yolanda is not the rabbit's foot holder, not the April leaver, not the February leaver.
% Therefore, Yolanda must be the one with the rabbit's foot OR the April leaver OR the February leaver — but not all.
% But the clue says the set of fliers is exactly these four, so each role corresponds to one flier.
% So: The rabbit's foot holder ≠ Yolanda, April leaver ≠ Yolanda, February leaver ≠ Yolanda.
% Therefore, Yolanda is not the rabbit's foot holder, not the April leaver, not the February leaver.
% So Yolanda must be the remaining one — i.e., the one with no other role.
% But the clue says the four fliers are: rabbit's foot, April, Yolanda, February.
% So Yolanda is one of them — and the others are the rabbit's foot holder, April leaver, February leaver.
% So: Yolanda is not the rabbit's foot holder, not the April leaver, not the February leaver.
% So: assign(Yolanda, M, C) implies M ≠ 4 and M ≠ 2 and C ≠ "rabbit's foot".
% But we can express this via constraints:
F1!=F2 :- assign(F1,M1,C1), assign(F2,M2,C2), F1="Yolanda", C2="rabbit's foot".
F1!=F2 :- assign(F1,M1,C1), assign(F2,M2,C2), F1="Yolanda", M2=4.
F1!=F2 :- assign(F1,M1,C1), assign(F2,M2,C2), F1="Yolanda", M2=2.

% 3. The aerophobe leaving in March is either the aerophobe with the wishbone or the passenger with the talisman.
{C="wishbone"; C="talisman"}=1 :- assign(F,M,C), M=3.

% 4. The passenger with the rabbit's foot will leave 1 month before the passenger with the coin.
M1 = M2 - 1 :- assign(F1,M1,C1), assign(F2,M2,C2), C1="rabbit's foot", C2="coin".

% 5. Neal will leave in February.
M=2 :- assign(F,M,C), F="Neal".
